title: 10.4 — Narrowing conversions, list initialization, and constexpr initializers

list: From a floating point type to an integral type.From a floating point type to a narrower or lesser ranked floating point type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and is in range of the destination type (even if the destination type doesn’t have the precision to store all the significant digits of the number).From an integral to a floating point type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and whose value can be stored exactly in the destination type.From an integral type to another integral type that cannot represent all values of the original type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and whose value can be stored exactly in the destination type. This covers both wider to narrower integral conversions, as well as integral sign conversions (signed to unsigned, or vice-versa).

From a floating point type to a narrower or lesser ranked floating point type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and is in range of the destination type (even if the destination type doesn’t have the precision to store all the significant digits of the number).From an integral to a floating point type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and whose value can be stored exactly in the destination type.From an integral type to another integral type that cannot represent all values of the original type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and whose value can be stored exactly in the destination type. This covers both wider to narrower integral conversions, as well as integral sign conversions (signed to unsigned, or vice-versa).

From an integral to a floating point type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and whose value can be stored exactly in the destination type.From an integral type to another integral type that cannot represent all values of the original type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and whose value can be stored exactly in the destination type. This covers both wider to narrower integral conversions, as well as integral sign conversions (signed to unsigned, or vice-versa).

From an integral type to another integral type that cannot represent all values of the original type, unless the value being converted is constexpr and whose value can be stored exactly in the destination type. This covers both wider to narrower integral conversions, as well as integral sign conversions (signed to unsigned, or vice-versa).

content.text: Best practiceBecause they can be unsafe and are a source of errors, avoid narrowing conversions whenever possible.
Because they can be unsafe and are a source of errors, avoid narrowing conversions whenever possible.
Best practiceIf you need to perform a narrowing conversion, usestatic_castto convert it into an explicit conversion.
If you need to perform a narrowing conversion, usestatic_castto convert it into an explicit conversion.

content.code: error C2397: conversion from 'double' to 'int' requires a narrowing conversion
int n { 5.0 }; // compile error: narrowing conversion

constexpr double d { 0.1 };
float f { d }; // not narrowing, even though loss of precision results

content.text: WarningConversion from a constexpr floating point type to a narrower floating point type is not considered narrowing even when a loss of precision results.
Conversion from a constexpr floating point type to a narrower floating point type is not considered narrowing even when a loss of precision results.

list: Having to use literal suffixes in most casesHaving to clutter our initializations with a static_cast
Having to clutter our initializations with a static_cast

content.text: Key insightFloating point types are ranked in this order (greater to lesser):Long doubleDoubleFloat
Floating point types are ranked in this order (greater to lesser):Long doubleDoubleFloat

However, your compiler may still issue a warning in this case (GCC and Clang do if you use the -Wconversion compile flag).Next lesson10.5Arithmetic conversionsBack to table of contentsPrevious lesson10.3Numeric conversions
